- Projection Layer — constant witnesses (web/constant-witness.js): π derived from D+/D- Polybius diagonal chiral carry (64-iteration alternating series, normalized to π); √3 from tetrahedral centroid-to-vertex squared relation (protocol stores 3, sqrt at projection boundary); φ from {3,5}/{5,3} dual incidence recurrence x[n+1]=1+1/x[n] (20 iterations, convergence check < 1e-12)
- Projection Layer — face panel UI (web/viewer/face-panel.js): FacePanel class — per-face cards with category badge (color-coded), role/edge/receipt fields; reduction chain display with step arrows; constant witness block showing π/√3/φ with derivation lane, checks, and convergence badge
- Web UI integration (web/index.html, web/app.js, web/style.css): 3D viewer panel (toggle hidden with #btn-toggle-viewer), Three.js r128 + inline OrbitControls, viewer scripts loaded in dependency order, viewer stats (cycle/receipts/filled), Smith chart overlay (140×140 semi-transparent), ring bar track, header controls, viewer panel visibility classes
- All 179 tests pass across 6 test suites (39+31+21+43+23+22), zero warnings
In Progress
- (none)
Blocked
- (none)
Key Decisions
- Opcode field = env->target[0] & 0x1F — keeps gauge0..7 canonical for transport sync
- Auto-dispatch mode: StreamParser.auto_dispatch = 1 triggers stream_execute() on envelope completion
- Non-printing semantics: gauge_exec_set_semantics() copies a new gauge table entry at runtime — same bytes, different behavior
- car/cdr chain evaluation depth-limited to 16 (prevents infinite loops)
- PROBE handshake: PROBE→PROBE_ACK with 32-bit capability bitmap → SYNC_COMMIT with ISA subset intersection
- Audio packed into relation32 field (16 × 16-bit PCM per envelope) — orientation0=0x0A, orientation1=0x01 marks audio frame
- Mesh: orientation0==0x01=route update, 0x02=data, 0x03=status, 0x04=RREQ, 0x05=RREP — no new opcode needed
- Mesh route propagation via periodic tick (30s default), not immediate re-flood — avoids loops
- Store-and-forward: 5s retry interval, max 3 retries, TTL decremented per hop (default 15)
- omicron_slide() works at gauge level via gauge_exec_eval — accessible through existing GAUGE_INVOKE opcode; no ISA change needed
- SLIDE opcode not wired into dispatch — unnecessary since gauge-level access suffices
- Geometry is incidence-based (no floating point at protocol level); coordinates only at projection/rendering boundary
- CIDR-as-CONS unifies factorial layers into longest-prefix selection function; OMIOM resolution = normalization of existing modulo/hash-based routing
- ViewerBridge extracts 5 Hopf parameters from envelope via gauge0..5/orientation0, mirroring omicron_resolve() modulo decomposition — chart11 = (gauge0^orientation0+gauge4)%11, baseQ = gauge1&3, fiberQ = gauge2&3, fano7 = gauge3%7, role3 = (gauge4+gauge5)%3
- Face resolution from gauge action (0x00→Omi-Form, 0x1C/0x1D→Omi-Glyph, 0x1E/0x1F→Omi-Map, 0x20→Omi-Matrix) plus orientation markers (0x0A→Omi-Sound, 0x0B→Omi-Light, 0x01→Omi-Mesh) plus gauge table activity (0xF0+→Omi-Receipt)
- Constants derived at projection boundary: π via Polybius D+/D- diagonal chiral carry (64-iteration alternating series, normalized, convergence check bounds 3.0, 3.3), √3 from tetrahedral centroid-to-vertex squared relation (protocol stores 3, sqrt at boundary), φ via {3,5}/{5,3} incidence recurrence x[n+1]=1+1/x[n] (20 iterations, convergence delta < 1e-12)
- Smith chart gamma computed from Hopf map components via stereographic projection: gr = a/(1+|c|), gi = b/(1+|c|), impedance z = (1-|γ|²)/((1-gr)²+gi²) + j(2gi)/((1-gr)²+gi²)
- Incidence-first gate: bridge checks gauge0 >= 0xF0 (canonical pre-header validity) before allowing geometric projection through resolveHopfQuquartRoute()
Next Steps
- Build dedicated OMIOM prefix-scoped cascade resolver (omi_omion.h/c) — or treat as normalization of existing omicron_resolve() modulo operations
- Add META64 receipt verification (omi_receipt.h/c)
- Test ESP32-S3 firmware build with mesh + CAD + CRC integration
- Deploy mesh debug status handler in firmware to respond to MESH_STATUS (0x03) requests from Web UI
- Verify face→role→edge→receipt-path reduction chain renders correctly in browser for all envelope types
- Add face filter/search UI to FacePanel for inspecting specific faces from the ~80-entry taxonomy
- Implement constant witness convergence indicators live in viewer (π limit convergence, φ recurrence delta) as visual projection boundary markers
Critical Context
- omi_env_validate() checks gauge0..7 against canonical pre-header (0xFF 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FF) — gauge0 must be >= 0xF0
- 6 control bytes (00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20) are the pre-language pleth: NUL=origin, FS=scope, GS=group, RS=record, US=unit, SP=surface
- Four gauge actions map to 4-type grammar: 0x00=PLACE (o---o), 0x1C/0x1D=REGISTER_INJECT (/---/), 0x1E/0x1F=KERNEL_READ (?---?), 0x20=RECORD_CLOSE (@---@)
- omicron_resolve(): route9 from env->gauge4..5 (S2), declaration10 from gauge6..7 (S3), witness11 from orientation0..1 (S4), phase12 from orientation2..3 (S5)
- Mesh route update format (0x01): target1=origin_id, target2=seqno, relation[] = packed {node_id, next_hop, metric, seqno} × up to 7 entries
- ESP32-S3 firmware: mesh-wraps LoRa transport; MAC-byte node_id; esp_timer_get_time()/1000 for mesh time
- stream.c depends on omi_dispatch.h — any target linking stream.o must also link omi_dispatch.o
- WASM build requires emcc; make wasm target compiles OMI C stack to web/omi_wasm.js + web/omi_wasm.wasm
- Firmware pin mapping (default): SX1262 on SPI2: CS=10, BUSY=9, RESET=8, DIO1=7, TX_EN=6, RX_EN=5
- Face registry has ~80 entries across 9 categories, each with (name, role, edge, receiptPath) — reduction chain: (name . face) → (face . role) → (role . edge) → (edge . receipt-path) → accepted.<role>
- Constants lane order: finite relation first → projection boundary second → classical constant equality third — never stored as protocol literals
- π lane: D+/D- diagonal closure from Polybius cell (row, col) → chiral accumulator → alternating projection stream → checked real limit → π
- √3 lane: tetrahedral centroid-to-vertex squared = 3 → projection boundary sqrt → length witness
- φ lane: {3,5}/{5,3} dual incidence → r{3,5} common core → recurrence xn+1 = 1 + 1/xn → checked convergence → φ
- Viewer load order: THREE.js r128 CDN → OrbitControls inline → omi_wasm.js → omi_web_serial.js → omi_web_audio.js → viewer/geometry-decoder.js → viewer/twin-viewer.js → face-registry.js → constant-witness.js → viewer-bridge.js → viewer/face-panel.js → app.js
- 3D viewer toggle: hidden by default (viewer-panel-hidden), lazy-inits TwinViewer on first show, stats update live from bridge state
- TwinViewer has null-guarded DOM access: smith-canvas/smith-tooltip/twin-info/ring-bar all optional — core WebGL and 3D objects render without them
Relevant Files
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omicron.h/c: Omicron resolution layer — omicron_resolve(env, layer), omicron_slide(ctx, code), factorial layer derivation
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omienv.h/c: 512-bit envelope, bitboard, 128-entry gauge table, canonical pre-header, 6 control bytes
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omi_mesh.h/c: Mesh routing — routing table, store-and-forward queue, dead-letter queue, RREQ/RREP, periodic flood, transport wrapper
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/isa.h: 32-slot opcode enum (0x00 NOP..0x1F VM_ESCAPE)
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omi_dispatch.h/c: 32-slot dispatch table, OMI_DispatchContext, all opcode handlers
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omi_transport.h/c: Abstract OMI_Transport struct
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/gauge_exec.h/c: Gauge lambda eval engine, car/cdr chain
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omi_transport_lora.h/c: SX1262 config + CRC16-wrapped Linux SPI transport stub
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/cpu.h: OMI_CPU with radio VM state fields
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/omi_web_bridge.c: Emscripten WASM bindings
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/app.js: Application logic — serial connect, inject, audio, mesh debug, envelope projection (projectEnvelope()), viewer bridge wiring (viewerBridge.pushEnvelope()), toggle/close/stats for 3D viewer
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/index.html: Web UI — connection, inject, audio, mesh debug, Omicron projection panels, 3D viewer panel (toggle hidden), Three.js r128 + OrbitControls inline, viewer scripts loaded in dependency order
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/style.css: Dark terminal theme — .place/.inject/.query/.close border-left colors for 4-type projection, viewer panel layout (480px 3D container, semi-transparent Smith chart overlay), header controls, ring bar track, button styles
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/viewer-bridge.js: ViewerBridge — envelope→geometric projection bridge, extracts 5 Hopf parameters, calls resolveHopfQuquartRoute(), computes Smith chart data, maintains receipt state, feeds TwinViewer.update()
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/face-registry.js: Named face taxonomy (~80 entries, 9 categories) — gauge action→face map, orientation→face map, resolveFaces(), faceReductionChain() for name→face→role→edge→receipt display
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/constant-witness.js: π derivation from D+/D- Polybius chiral carry, √3 from tetrahedral squared relation, φ from {3,5}/{5,3} incidence recurrence — deriveConstants(polybiusRow, polybiusCol) returns all three with convergence checks
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/viewer/geometry-decoder.js: Pure JS mirror of omicron.c — resolveHopfQuquartRoute(), SOLID_DB (8 platonic solids), FANO_LINES, binary quadratic form, quaternion Hopf map, fetchSolid with local fallback
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/viewer/twin-viewer.js: TwinViewer class — WebGL 3D scene with Polybius grid (5×5), Fano plane (7 points, 7 lines in 3D), 5040-slot spiral helix, Hopf arrow vectors, Smith chart (impedance/admittance), solid geometry (8 solids), three.js r128, OrbitControls, animate loop
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/viewer/ring-loader.js: Data loader with WS/SSE/polling (included for standalone use, not needed for bridge mode)
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/web/viewer/face-panel.js: FacePanel class — face cards with category badges and reduction chain, constant witness blocks with π/√3/φ values, lanes, checks, and convergence indicators
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/firmware/main/main.cpp: ESP32-S3 entry — mesh-wrapped LoRa transport, CDC UART bridge
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/firmware/main/omi_sx1262.h/cpp: Real SX1262 SPI driver — TX, RX, CAD, IRQ, busy polling
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/firmware/main/omi_esp_transport.h/cpp: OMI_Transport impl for SX1262 + CAD listen-before-talk
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omi_transport_sim.h/c: Simulated paired transport with linked ring buffers
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/omi_probe.h/c: Probe handshake state machine
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/.github/workflows/ci.yml: GitHub Actions — test + WASM build
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/docs/: Spec docs — architecture, envelope-spec, isa-spec, mesh-protocol
- /home/main/omi/omi-isa/Makefile: Build rules — all targets, make test (6 suites), make wasm
- /home/main/omi/omi-tetragrammatron/docs/Omi Surface Projection Faces.md: Complete face taxonomy (~80 named faces), reduction chain semantics, face categories, cross-class mappings — validated and implemented in face-registry.js
- /home/main/omi/omi-tetragrammatron/docs/Constants as Projection Witnesses.md: π/√3/φ derivation lanes, protocol order (finite relation → projection boundary → classical equality), no stored constants — validated and implemented in constant-witness.js

▣ Build · Big Pickle · 44.1s
+ Thought: 3.6s
Goal
- Build a layered distributed semantic execution stack: 512-bit envelope carrier, 32-slot ISA dispatch, gauge lambda engine, abstract transport, probe handshake, LoRa RF, Web Serial bridge, ESP32-S3 firmware, audio capture/playback, mesh networking, Omicron CIDR resolution layer, projection layer with named face registry, constant derivation witnesses, incidence gate, and 3D WebGL viewer integration.
Constraints & Preferences
- C99 (-std=c99), gcc, minimal dependencies
- Binary 64-byte envelopes for sensor injection (not text)
- Non-printing semantics: gauge table IS the program; behavior changes without packet format change
- Transport layer never sees meaning — byte stream in/out only
- Opcode for dispatch stored in env->target[0] & 0x1F
- OMI-Lisp is declarative only, never authoritative state
- Web Serial API for browser-based runtime; WASM-compiled OMI stack
- Audio packed directly into relation[32] field (16 × 16-bit PCM samples per envelope)
- Four-phase meta-circular evaluator: o---o (PLACE) → /---/ (REGISTER_INJECT) → ?---? (KERNEL_READ) → @---@ (RECORD_CLOSE) — maps directly to gauge actions
- CIDR-as-CONS: address/prefix = (address . prefix); longest-prefix match = resolution
- Factorial tower: 8! physical frame, 9!–12! interpretive envelopes derived via omicron_resolve(), not separate wire fields
- Geometry is incidence-based (tetrahedra, Fano plane, Polybius grid) — no floating point at protocol level; coordinates only at projection boundary
- Faces are not modules: named faces do not multiply modules or create authority; they are readable surface names for accepted OMI objects
- Constants are never protocol literals: finite incidence/relation first → projection boundary second → classical constant equality third — constants are witnesses of projection, not authority inside the protocol
- Face registry reduction: (name . face) → (face . role) → (role . edge) → (edge . receipt-path) — only validation + receipt accepts the resolved relation
- Incidence-first gate: bridge validates canonical pre-header (gauge0 >= 0xF0) before allowing geometric projection through resolveHopfQuquartRoute()
Progress
Done
- Phase 0: 512-bit envelope parser + uniform bitboard + gauge table (omienv.h/c) — 39 tests
- Phase 0: Streaming parser state machine + binary sensor injection (stream.h/c)
- Phase 0: 512-byte sector iterator (8 cells) (sector.h/c)
- Phase 1: 32-slot opcode dispatch table (omi_dispatch.h/c) — 31 tests
- Phase 1: Abstract OMI_Transport struct + envelope send/recv helpers (omi_transport.h/c)
- Phase 1: Radio VM state fields in OMI_CPU (capability, peers, probe_state)
- Phase 2: Gauge lambda eval engine (gauge_exec.h/c) — depth-limited car/cdr chain (max 16) — 21 tests
- Phase 3: Simulated paired transport (omi_transport_sim.h/c) + probe handshake (omi_probe.h/c) — 43 tests
- Phase 3: SX1262 config + Linux SPI stub factory (omi_transport_lora.h/c)
- Phase 4: WASM bridge (web/omi_web_bridge.c) — Emscripten exports for push_byte, envelope polling, injectors
- Phase 4: Web Serial transport (web/omi_web_serial.js) — connect/disconnect, readLoop, sendEnvelope
- Phase 4: Web Audio capture/playback (web/omi_web_audio.js, web/omi_audio_worklet.js) — PCM 16-sample frames
- Phase 4: Web UI (web/index.html, web/app.js, web/style.css) — status bar, serial connect, inject, audio VU, envelope log
- Phase 4: Makefile wasm target — emcc compile of OMI C stack
- Phase 5: Real SX1262 SPI driver (firmware/main/omi_sx1262.h/cpp) — TX, RX, CAD, IRQ, busy polling
- Phase 5: ESP transport (firmware/main/omi_esp_transport.h/cpp) — OMI_Transport impl wrapping SX1262
- Phase 5: ESP32-S3 firmware (firmware/main/main.cpp) — mesh-wrapped LoRa transport, CDC UART bridge
- Phase 5: Build system (firmware/main/CMakeLists.txt, firmware/CMakeLists.txt, firmware/platformio.ini)
- Phase 6: Audio envelope detection + playback in app.js — orientation0==0x0A marker, 16×16-bit PCM from relation32..63
- Phase 7: Mesh routing layer (omi_mesh.h/c) — routing table (max 32), store-and-forward queue (max 64), DSDV-style flood, dead-letter queue (max 32), RREQ/RREP on-demand routing — 23 tests
- Phase 7: Mesh ESP32 integration — main.cpp wraps LoRa transport with omi_mesh_create(), MAC-byte node_id
- Phase 7: CRC16 for LoRa transport (omi_transport_lora.c) — CCITT CRC-16 appended to TX, verified on RX
- Phase 7: SX1262 CAD (omi_sx1262.h/cpp) — sx1262_set_cad_params() + sx1262_cad(), listen-before-talk in esp_send()
- Phase 7: Web UI mesh debug panel — route count, queue depth, dead letter count, auto-refresh (5s)
- Phase 7: Open-source packaging — LICENSE (MIT), CONTRIBUTING.md, .github/workflows/ci.yml, docs/{architecture,envelope-spec,isa-spec,mesh-protocol}.md
- Omicron Resolution Layer (omicron.h/c): omicron_resolve(env, layer) derives any factorial layer (8!–12!) from envelope S-segments; omicron_slide(ctx, code) runs 4-phase meta-circular interpreter (PLACE→REGISTER_INJECT→KERNEL_READ→RECORD_CLOSE) — 22 tests
- Mesh markers renumbered: RREQ→0x04, RREP→0x05, data→0x02, MESH_STATUS→0x03
- DOM projection (web/app.js): projectEnvelope() maps gauge action → CSS class (place/inject/query/close) + address + receipt
- Projection Layer — viewer integration (web/viewer/geometry-decoder.js): JS mirror of omicron.c — resolveHopfQuquartRoute(), SOLID_DB (8 platonic solids), FANO_LINES, binary quadratic form, quaternion Hopf map
- Projection Layer — WebGL viewer (web/viewer/twin-viewer.js): TwinViewer class — Polybius grid (5×5), Fano plane (7 points, 7 lines in 3D), 5040-slot spiral helix, Hopf arrow vectors, Smith chart (impedance/admittance), solid geometry (8 solids via fetchSolid fallback), three.js r128 WebGL rendering
- Projection Layer — bridge (web/viewer-bridge.js): ViewerBridge — incidence validation gate (validateIncidence() checks gauge0..7 against 0xFF 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FF), gauge action extraction, face resolution via resolveFaces(), constant derivation via deriveConstants(), 5 Hopf parameter extraction (chart11 from gauge0^orientation0+gauge4%11, baseQ/fiberQ from gauge1..2&0x03, fano7 from gauge3%7, role3 from gauge4+gauge5%3), calls resolveHopfQuquartRoute() for full geometric projection, computes Smith chart data (gamma from stereographic projection, impedance/admittance), builds receipts with face info + constants, maintains accumulated state (max 500), feeds TwinViewer.update()
- Projection Layer — named face registry (web/face-registry.js): ~80 entries across 9 categories (core, authority, scope, runtime, function, geometry, carrier, hardware, graph) — each with (name, role, edge, receiptPath); gauge action→primary face map (0x00→Omi-Form, 0x1C/0x1D→Omi-Glyph, 0x1E/0x1F→Omi-Map, 0x20→Omi-Matrix); orientation marker→secondary face map (0x01→Omi-Mesh, 0x0A→Omi-Sound, 0x0B→Omi-Light); faceReductionChain() displays full name→face→role→edge→receipt chain
- Projection Layer — constant witnesses (web/constant-witness.js): π derived from D+/D- Polybius diagonal chiral carry (64-iteration alternating series, normalized to π, convergence check bounds 3.0, 3.3); √3 from tetrahedral centroid-to-vertex squared relation (protocol stores 3, sqrt at projection boundary); φ from {3,5}/{5,3} dual incidence recurrence x[n+1]=1+1/x[n] (20 iterations, convergence delta < 1e-12); deriveConstants(polybiusRow, polybiusCol) returns all three with convergence checks and witness metadata
- Projection Layer — face panel UI (web/viewer/face-panel.js): FacePanel class — per-face cards with category badge (color-coded: core/authority/scope/runtime/function/geometry/carrier/hardware/graph), role/edge/receipt fields; reduction chain display with → step arrows; constant witness block showing π/√3/φ with value, derivation lane, 2-3 checks per constant, and convergence/diverged badge
- Web UI integration (web/index.html, web/app.js, web/style.css): 3D viewer panel (toggle hidden with #btn-toggle-viewer), Three.js r128 + inline OrbitControls, viewer scripts loaded in dependency order (geometry-decoder → twin-viewer → face-registry → constant-witness → face-panel → viewer-bridge → app), viewer stats (cycle/receipts/filled), Smith chart overlay (140×140 semi-transparent), ring bar track, face panel overlay (220px left sidebar with blur backdrop), header controls, viewer panel visibility classes
- All 179 tests pass across 6 test suites (39+31+21+43+23+22), zero warnings

Key Decisions
- Opcode field = env->target[0] & 0x1F — keeps gauge0..7 canonical for transport sync
- Auto-dispatch mode: StreamParser.auto_dispatch = 1 triggers stream_execute() on envelope completion
- Non-printing semantics: gauge_exec_set_semantics() copies a new gauge table entry at runtime — same bytes, different behavior
- car/cdr chain evaluation depth-limited to 16 (prevents infinite loops)
- PROBE handshake: PROBE→PROBE_ACK with 32-bit capability bitmap → SYNC_COMMIT with ISA subset intersection
- Audio packed into relation32 field (16 × 16-bit PCM per envelope) — orientation0=0x0A, orientation1=0x01 marks audio frame
- Mesh: orientation0==0x01=route update, 0x02=data, 0x03=status, 0x04=RREQ, 0x05=RREP — no new opcode needed
- Mesh route propagation via periodic tick (30s default), not immediate re-flood — avoids loops
- Store-and-forward: 5s retry interval, max 3 retries, TTL decremented per hop (default 15)
- omicron_slide() works at gauge level via gauge_exec_eval — accessible through existing GAUGE_INVOKE opcode; no ISA change needed
- Geometry is incidence-based (no floating point at protocol level); coordinates only at projection/rendering boundary
- CIDR-as-CONS unifies factorial layers into longest-prefix selection function; OMIOM resolution = normalization of existing modulo/hash-based routing
- ViewerBridge extracts 5 Hopf parameters from envelope via gauge0..5/orientation0, mirroring omicron_resolve() modulo decomposition — chart11 = (gauge0^orientation0+gauge4)%11, baseQ = gauge1&3, fiberQ = gauge2&3, fano7 = gauge3%7, role3 = (gauge4+gauge5)%3
- Face resolution from gauge action (0x00→Omi-Form, 0x1C/0x1D→Omi-Glyph, 0x1E/0x1F→Omi-Map, 0x20→Omi-Matrix) plus orientation markers (0x0A→Omi-Sound, 0x0B→Omi-Light, 0x01→Omi-Mesh) plus gauge table activity (0xF0+→Omi-Receipt)
- Constants derived at projection boundary: π via Polybius D+/D- diagonal chiral carry (64-iteration alternating series, normalized, convergence check bounds 3.0, 3.3), √3 from tetrahedral centroid-to-vertex squared relation (protocol stores 3, sqrt at boundary), φ via {3,5}/{5,3} incidence recurrence x[n+1]=1+1/x[n] (20 iterations, convergence delta < 1e-12)
- Smith chart gamma computed from Hopf map components via stereographic projection: gr = a/(1+|c|), gi = b/(1+|c|), impedance z = (1-|γ|²)/((1-gr)²+gi²) + j(2gi)/((1-gr)²+gi²)
- Incidence-first gate: bridge checks env0..7 against canonical pre-header (0xFF 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FF) before allowing geometric projection through resolveHopfQuquartRoute() — validates finite relation first, projects second
- Face panel overlay positioned as left sidebar within viewer wrapper (absolute, z-index 5, 220px, blur backdrop) — non-blocking pointer events
- Constants lane order in UI: finite relation first (lane label) → projection boundary step → classical constant equality — matches protocol order

Critical Context
- omi_env_validate() checks gauge0..7 against canonical pre-header (0xFF 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FF) — gauge0 must be >= 0xF0; bridge mirrors this in validateIncidence()
- 6 control bytes (00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20) are the pre-language pleth: NUL=origin, FS=scope, GS=group, RS=record, US=unit, SP=surface
- Four gauge actions map to 4-type grammar: 0x00=PLACE (o---o), 0x1C/0x1D=REGISTER_INJECT (/---/), 0x1E/0x1F=KERNEL_READ (?---?), 0x20=RECORD_CLOSE (@---@)
- omicron_resolve(): route9 from env->gauge4..5 (S2), declaration10 from gauge6..7 (S3), witness11 from orientation0..1 (S4), phase12 from orientation2..3 (S5)
- Mesh route update format (0x01): target1=origin_id, target2=seqno, relation[] = packed {node_id, next_hop, metric, seqno} × up to 7 entries
- ESP32-S3 firmware: mesh-wraps LoRa transport; MAC-byte node_id; esp_timer_get_time()/1000 for mesh time
- stream.c depends on omi_dispatch.h — any target linking stream.o must also link omi_dispatch.o
- WASM build requires emcc; make wasm target compiles OMI C stack to web/omi_wasm.js + web/omi_wasm.wasm
- Firmware pin mapping (default): SX1262 on SPI2: CS=10, BUSY=9, RESET=8, DIO1=7, TX_EN=6, RX_EN=5
- Face registry has ~80 entries across 9 categories, each with (name, role, edge, receiptPath) — reduction chain: (name . face) → (face . role) → (role . edge) → (edge . receipt-path) → accepted.<role>
- Constants lane order: finite relation first → projection boundary second → classical constant equality third — never stored as protocol literals
- π lane: D+/D- diagonal closure from Polybius cell (row, col) → chiral accumulator → alternating projection stream → checked real limit → π
- √3 lane: tetrahedral centroid-to-vertex squared = 3 → projection boundary sqrt → length witness
- φ lane: {3,5}/{5,3} dual incidence → r{3,5} common core → recurrence xn+1 = 1 + 1/xn → checked convergence → φ
- Viewer load order: THREE.js r128 CDN → OrbitControls inline → omi_wasm.js → omi_web_serial.js → omi_web_audio.js → viewer/geometry-decoder.js → viewer/twin-viewer.js → face-registry.js → constant-witness.js → viewer-bridge.js → viewer/face-panel.js → app.js
- 3D viewer toggle: hidden by default (viewer-panel-hidden), lazy-inits TwinViewer on first show, stats update live from bridge state
- TwinViewer has null-guarded DOM access: smith-canvas/smith-tooltip/twin-info/ring-bar all optional — core WebGL and 3D objects render without them
- Face panel (.face-card, .constants-block) styled with rgba background + backdrop-filter blur for overlay readability over 3D scene; color-coded category badges (9 variants); chain-step-* classes for reduction chain display; const-row with symbol/value/lane/checks layout
- ViewerBridge.processEnvelope() now returns buildFrameData() with twin.faces (resolved face objects) and twin.constants (π/√3/φ witnesses) in addition to existing twin geometry

`2026-07-05 23:01` | `id:260705-2301` ^260705-230115

You’ve effectively already “unbound it” in practice by splitting **implementation (distributed runtime + envelope + VM)** from **specification (docs + face registry + projection layer + omicron layer)**—so yes, that part is structurally decoupled.

But there’s a subtle distinction in what you’re describing that matters for your next step:

### 1. “Different file location” ≠ true spec separation
Right now, your system still behaves like a **tightly coupled interpreted machine**, because:
- `gauge table IS the program` (you explicitly state this)
- dispatch + semantics can change without packet format change
- projection layer *reads* execution meaning (faces, constants, geometry)

So even if the spec lives elsewhere, the runtime still *depends on semantic coupling*.

### 2. What “true unbinding” would look like in your architecture
If you actually want a clean separation for research / spec-building:

You would enforce a hard boundary like:

- **Layer A: Envelope spec (frozen)**
 - 512-bit format
 - gauge/orientation/target/relation layout
 - no semantics allowed here

- **Layer B: Execution semantics (pluggable)**
 - gauge table, dispatch, omicron rules
 - can evolve freely

- **Layer C: Projection / interpretation (non-authoritative)**
 - face registry
 - constants (π, φ, √3 witnesses)
 - WebGL / UI
 - explicitly *never feeds back into execution*

Right now, your system violates the last rule slightly because:
> projection layer influences how you reason about envelopes (even if not execution)

### 3. What your current move actually is (important framing)
What you’re really doing is not “unbinding” in a filesystem sense.

You’re building a:

> **dual-channel system: executable substrate + reflective semantic model**

That’s why it feels like “slide rules over circular helices” rather than a normal VM.

### 4. If your goal is to build a *clean spec from this*
Then the next structural step is:

- Extract a **formal ISA spec that does NOT mention:**
 - faces
 - constants derivation
 - projection geometry
 - mesh topology

- Keep only:
 - envelope bit layout
 - opcode semantics
 - state machine rules
 - transport behavior

Everything else becomes:
> “reference implementations and interpretive layers”

---

### 5. The key tension you’re hitting
Right now your system is simultaneously:
- a deterministic VM (tests confirm this)
- a semantic geometry engine (faces/constants/projection)

Those two are fine together—but only if you decide:

> Is geometry *descriptive tooling* or *semantic authority*?

Because right now it is slightly both.

---

If you want, I can help you split this into a clean **3-document spec set**:

1. `OMI-ISA.md` (pure machine)
2. `OMI-Transport.md` (radio/mesh/web/ESP32)
3. `OMI-Projection.md` (faces, constants, WebGL, Omicron interpretation)

That would fully formalize the “unbinding” you’re aiming at.

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-05 23:05` | `id:260705-2305` ^260705-230556

What you’re describing is basically a **“canonical perception + rendering prior layer”**—a system that sits *above raw geometry* and *below interpretation*, where diagrams are no longer ad-hoc drawings but **parameterized, bounded, and spectrum-controlled templates**.

If we translate your phrasing into a clean technical model, it becomes something like this:

---

## 1. Predefined diagram configurations (template layer)

Instead of “drawing a diagram,” you define:

> **Diagram = parameterized generative schema**

Each schema includes:
- **Topology class** (graph, lattice, incidence structure, flow field, mesh, etc.)
- **Canonical anchor points** (fixed reference geometry)
- **Allowed transformations** (rotation, scaling, deformation, relabeling)
- **Semantic slots** (nodes/edges/regions with typed meaning)
- **Rendering contract** (how it must appear visually)

So a “diagram” is no longer an image—it’s:

> `(template_id + parameter vector + constraint set) → deterministic structure`

---

## 2. Pattern-descriptive tooling (semantic grammar layer)

This is where your system becomes interesting.

You’re not just drawing—you’re defining a **diagram grammar**:

- Node patterns: chain, star, ring, bipartite, hypergraph
- Edge patterns: directed flow, symmetric coupling, constraint binding
- Field patterns: gradients, phase fields, discrete incidence, lattice folds

So instead of:
> “draw a network”

You specify:
> “instantiate STAR_PATTERN with 6 leaves, constraint symmetry = 0.8, flow bias = clockwise”

This turns diagrams into **compiled visual programs**.

---

## 3. Spectral bounding (frequency-domain constraint layer)

This is your “calibration layer.”

You enforce that any diagram must satisfy constraints in transform space:

### Examples:
- Spatial frequency limit (no detail above threshold k_max)
- Structural spectrum (graph Laplacian eigenvalue bounds)
- Complexity bandwidth (number of active harmonics / features)
- Noise floor separation (signal vs interpretive artifacts)

So a diagram is valid only if:

> `S(geometry) ∈ allowed spectral envelope`

This prevents:
- over-dense representations
- ambiguous visual noise
- unbounded recursion in structure

Think of it as:
> “Nyquist rule for diagrams + topology stability filter”

---

## 4. Constraint ratios (structural invariants layer)

This is where meaning becomes *stable under transformation*.

You define ratios that must remain invariant:

- node:edge ratio bounds
- symmetry vs asymmetry ratio
- density per unit area
- branching factor constraints
- entropy distribution across regions

So diagrams are not just valid visually—they are:

> **structurally conserved under deformation**

This gives you:
- calibration consistency
- cross-render comparability
- machine-verifiable diagram equivalence

---

## 5. Putting it together (your full stack, normalized)

You are effectively defining:

> **Canonical Computer Vision Diagram Compiler**

Pipeline:

```
Template Schema
 ↓
Pattern Grammar Instantiation
 ↓
Constraint Solver (ratios + invariants)
 ↓
Spectral Bounding Filter (frequency / topology limits)
 ↓
Deterministic Rendering / Projection
```

And optionally:
```
↓
Interpretation Layer (semantic tagging, faces, meaning)
```

---

## 6. The key conceptual shift you’re aiming at

You’re not building “better diagrams.”

You’re building:

> **diagrams that behave like physical systems under constraint**

Meaning:
- they have conservation laws (ratios)
- they have bandwidth limits (spectral bounds)
- they have allowed state spaces (templates)
- they evolve under transformation rules (pattern grammar)

---

## 7. How this would plug into your existing OMI-style system

If you align it with what you already built:

- **Envelope layer** → raw structured event/data
- **Gauge layer** → transformation semantics
- **Projection layer** → face/geometry rendering
- **This new layer** → *diagram physics engine*

It would sit right above your projection layer and act as:

> “what shapes are even allowed to exist before they are interpreted”

---

If you want, next step I can help you formalize this into a real spec:

- `diagram-template.json` schema
- spectral constraint DSL
- ratio invariant definitions
- or a minimal C/WASM runtime for generating these diagrams deterministically

Just say which direction you want to push it.

## You
`2026-07-05 23:10` | `id:260705-2310` ^260705-231056

you can thiotafrnk of 32 place value chracters in an omi---imo notation citation insteadn of betti and schalifi just thaat each nibble can be a point or line like n-ball or monoid # OMI Version Control from Place-Value Notation, Betti–Schläfli Interpretation, and Seed Objects

## 1. The Core Claim

OMI version control is not merely a history of text edits.

It is a history of scoped declarations moving through:

```text
place
 ↓
citation
 ↓
validation
 ↓
receipt
 ↓
projection
```

A normal version-control system asks:

```text
What changed in this file?
```

OMI version control asks:

```text
What relation changed?
Where was it scoped?
Which carrier held it?
Which seed interpreted it?
Which topology did it project through?
Which receipt accepted it?
```

This means the version is not just the file state.

The version is the accepted receipt of a scoped transformation.

---

# 2. Place-Value Notation as Version Scope

OMI begins with place value.

The 32 non-printing controls stage 32 address places:

```text
0x00..0x1F → n00..n31
```

These are not readable text yet.

They are the place controllers.

Readable notation appears later.

So every version begins with the question:

```text
Which place changed?
```

Not merely:

```text
Which character changed?
```

A versioned object is therefore a place-value object:

```text
n00 n01 n02 n03
n04 n05 n06 n07
n08 n09 n10 n11
n12 n13 n14 n15
n16 n17 n18 n19
n20 n21 n22 n23
n24 n25 n26 n27
n28 n29 n30 n31
```

These 32 places group into the OMI ruler:

```text
S0-S1-S2-S3/S4/S5/S6/S7
```

So a version can be addressed as:

```text
file changed at the byte level
 ↓
declaration changed at the notation level
 ↓
place-value cascade changed at the OMI level
 ↓
receipt changed at the validation level
```

This gives a deeper version identity than a line diff.

---

# 3. FS/GS/RS/US as Version-Control Topology

The final four ASCII controls form the topology spine:

```text
FS = File Separator
GS = Group Separator
RS = Record Separator
US = Unit Separator
```

In OMI version control, these become scope boundaries:

```text
FS = file version scope
GS = group/module version scope
RS = record/declaration version scope
US = unit/value version scope
```

So instead of only naming files, OMI names where inside the file the version belongs.

```text
fs.o/project
 ↓
gs.o/skills
 ↓
rs.o/base64-seed
 ↓
us.o/alphabet
```

This is CIDR-like because each prefix narrows the valid interpretation.

Example:

```text
fs.o/SKILLS.md
fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding
fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64
fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64/us.o/alphabet
```

A change at `us.o/alphabet` is smaller than a change at `rs.o/base64`.

A change at `rs.o/base64` is smaller than a change at `gs.o/encoding`.

A change at `fs.o/SKILLS.md` changes the whole file scope.

This lets version control become scoped.

---

# 4. `o.o` as the Neutral Version Seed

The origin object is:

```text
o.o
```

This is the neutral seed.

It means:

```text
origin object
neutral closure
no specialized carrier interpretation yet
```

Every scoped encoding model can specialize `o.o`.

Examples:

```text
o.base64.o
o.hex.o
o.binary.o
o.utf8.o
o.jabcode.o
o.ipv6.o
```

Each one means:

```text
take the neutral origin object
scope it through a named interpretation
close it back into object form
```

So:

```text
o.base64.o
```

means:

```text
the Base64-scoped neutral seed object
```

It is not a payload.

It is not a decoded value.

It is the declared interpretation environment for payloads that will later arrive.

---

# 5. `omi-<base>-<64>-imo` as Citation Route

The matching citation route is:

```text
omi-base-64-imo
```

This says:

```text
OMI citation side
 ↓
base interpretation
 ↓
64-radix carrier parameter
 ↓
IMO carrier side
```

So the relationship is:

```text
o.base64.o
 = neutral seed object

omi-base-64-imo
 = citation route for Base64 carrier interpretation
```

Together:

```lisp
(o.base64.o . omi-base-64-imo)
```

means:

```text
the Base64 seed object is cited through the OMI/IMO Base64 route
```

This is perfect for version control because it separates:

```text
encoding scope
carrier route
payload instance
validation receipt
```

A file version may carry a Base64 string, but the version-control system knows whether that string was interpreted under `o.base64.o`.

---

# 6. Zero Polynomial Function Encoding Schema

The zero-polynomial seed means:

```text
no payload deformation has been accepted yet
only the interpretation scope has been declared
```

For Base64:

```text
o.base64.o
```

can be treated as:

```text
zero polynomial over Base64 carrier scope
```

Meaning:

```text
f₀(x) = 0 under Base64 interpretation context
```

This is not arithmetic pretending to be decoding.

It is a neutral function seed.

It says:

```text
This encoding scope exists.
No payload has yet been accepted through it.
```

Then an actual payload enters:

```text
encoded bytes
 ↓
omi-base-64-imo
 ↓
validate Base64 grammar
 ↓
decode through skill.base64.decode
 ↓
receipt accepts decoded relation
```

The seed is versioned separately from the payload.

This matters because the algorithm may evolve.

Version control must distinguish:

```text
Base64 schema changed
payload changed
decode algorithm changed
receipt changed
projection changed
```

---

# 7. Betti–Schläfli Interpretation

A version is not only textual.

It can also project as topology.

The Betti–Schläfli layer asks:

```text
What kind of shape does this accepted relation project as?
What holes, boundaries, shells, or incidence forms does it imply?
```

Schläfli symbols describe regular geometric structure:

```text
{3,3} tetrahedral relation
{4,3} cubical relation
{3,4} octahedral relation
{5,3} dodecahedral relation
{3,5} icosahedral relation
```

Betti numbers describe topological features:

```text
b0 = connected components
b1 = loops / tunnels
b2 = voids / enclosed shells
```

So a version can carry both:

```text
place-value address
receipt hash
scope prefix
encoding seed
Schläfli projection
Betti signature
```

Example:

```lisp
(version.seed . o.base64.o)
(version.route . omi-base-64-imo)
(version.scope . fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64)
(version.schlafli . "{3,3}")
(version.betti . "(1 0 0)")
```

That says:

```text
this version of the Base64 seed is a connected, no-hole, no-void seed relation
```

If a later version introduces a cyclic dependency, it may project differently:

```lisp
(version.betti . "(1 1 0)")
```

meaning:

```text
one connected component
one loop
no enclosed void
```

This gives version control a topology of change.

---

# 8. Why This Is Better Than Plain Git Diff

Git records file snapshots and diffs.

OMI version control records scoped accepted relations.

Git can say:

```text
line 42 changed
```

OMI can say:

```text
the Base64 seed object's alphabet unit changed
under FS/GS/RS/US scope
inside SKILLS.md
using omi-base-64-imo carrier route
validated against skill.base64.alphabet
accepted into receipt slot N
projected with Betti signature (1,0,0)
```

Git tracks text.

OMI tracks relation acceptance.

The systems can work together:

```text
Git commit
 = carrier snapshot

OMI receipt
 = accepted semantic relation inside the snapshot
```

So Git remains useful.

OMI augments it with deterministic semantic versioning.

---

# 9. Version-Control Objects

An OMI version object should include:

```text
version id
parent receipt
scope prefix
seed object
citation route
carrier file
declaration hash
algorithm hash
validation rule
receipt handle
projection signature
Betti numbers
Schläfli symbol
logical cycle
```

Readable declaration:

```lisp
(version.id . receipt-042)
(version.parent . receipt-041)
(version.scope . fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64/us.o/alphabet)
(version.seed . o.base64.o)
(version.route . omi-base-64-imo)
(version.carrier . SKILLS.md)
(version.algorithm . skill.base64.decode)
(version.validation . tetragrammatron.validate)
(version.schlafli . "{3,3}")
(version.betti . "(1 0 0)")
(version.result . accepted)
```

This lets a peer reconstruct the meaning of the version without trusting the original machine.

---

# 10. AGENTS.md and SKILLS.md in Version Control

`AGENTS.md` versions the allowed behavior.

`SKILLS.md` versions the algorithms.

`.imo` files carry normalized declarations.

Receipts accept exact versions.

So:

```text
AGENTS.md
 controls resolver behavior

SKILLS.md
 defines reproducible algorithms

*.imo
 carries normalized scoped declarations

receipt ring
 records accepted versions
```

Example:

```lisp
(agent.allowed-skill . skill.base64.decode)
(agent.version-scope . fs.o/AGENTS.md/gs.o/permissions)

(skill.base64.decode.seed . o.base64.o)
(skill.base64.decode.route . omi-base-64-imo)
(skill.base64.decode.effect . pure)
(skill.base64.decode.version . v1)
```

A change to AGENTS.md may affect whether a skill is allowed.

A change to SKILLS.md may affect how the skill computes.

A change to `.imo` may affect the normalized carrier.

A receipt identifies which exact combination was accepted.

---

# 11. Declarative Version Transitions

A version transition is just a declared relation:

```lisp
(version.previous . receipt-041)
(version.next . receipt-042)
(version.delta . changed-base64-alphabet)
```

But it does not become accepted merely because the declaration exists.

It follows the pipeline:

```text
version delta declaration
 ↓
citation
 ↓
scope resolution
 ↓
algorithm validation
 ↓
receipt
 ↓
projection
```

That gives lazy version control.

A proposed version can exist without being accepted.

A branch can exist as candidates.

A merge can exist as a declared relation.

Only validation decides what enters the accepted receipt history.

---

# 12. Branches as Scope Forks

A branch is a scoped fork of receipt lineage.

```lisp
(branch.name . feature/base64-seed)
(branch.from . receipt-041)
(branch.scope . fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64)
```

The branch does not corrupt main history because it is scoped.

```text
main receipt line
 receipt-040 → receipt-041

branch receipt line
 receipt-041 → candidate-042a → receipt-043a
```

A merge becomes:

```lisp
(merge.left . receipt-043a)
(merge.right . receipt-044b)
(merge.scope . fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding)
```

Validation checks whether the merged relation is consistent.

If accepted, the merge gets a receipt.

---

# 13. Merges as Topological Gluing

This is where Betti–Schläfli interpretation becomes powerful.

A merge is not just text conflict resolution.

It is gluing relation surfaces.

If two branches edit disjoint units:

```text
branch A edits us.o/alphabet
branch B edits us.o/padding
```

then the merge may preserve simple topology:

```text
Betti = (1,0,0)
```

If two branches introduce circular dependency:

```text
skill.A depends on skill.B
skill.B depends on skill.A
```

then the merge may produce a loop:

```text
Betti = (1,1,0)
```

If a set of dependencies encloses a missing unresolved declaration, it may produce a void:

```text
Betti = (1,1,1)
```

This lets version control detect not only textual conflict, but structural conflict.

Schläfli projection can label the shape of the accepted relation:

```text
{3,3} minimal seed
{4,3} cubical dependency lattice
{3,4} octahedral reciprocal relation
{5,3}/{3,5} higher semantic shells
```

This is not replacing tests.

It augments tests with topology.

---

# 14. Seed Objects as Versioned Base Models

A seed object like:

```text
o.base64.o
```

acts like a base model.

It defines the neutral interpretation surface.

Other versions specialize it:

```text
o.base64.rfc4648.o
o.base64.urlsafe.o
o.base64.nopad.o
```

Each one can cite a route:

```text
omi-base-64-imo
omi-base-64-url-imo
omi-base-64-nopad-imo
```

Each one can have a skill:

```lisp
(skill.base64.rfc4648.decode . algorithm.base64.rfc4648)
(skill.base64.urlsafe.decode . algorithm.base64.urlsafe)
(skill.base64.nopad.decode . algorithm.base64.nopad)
```

This turns encoding schemas into versioned objects.

The seed says:

```text
what interpretation family is this?
```

The route says:

```text
how does it travel between OMI and IMO?
```

The skill says:

```text
how is it computed exactly?
```

The receipt says:

```text
which instance was accepted?
```

---

# 15. Version IDs as OMI Receipts

A version ID should not be only a hash of file text.

It should be a receipt handle.

Example:

```text
receipt:5040/2820
```

or:

```text
omi-version:fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64@receipt-2820
```

This identifies:

```text
scope
carrier
relation
receipt
```

A peer can resolve:

```text
Which file?
Which group?
Which record?
Which unit?
Which accepted receipt?
```

This is better than a plain hash because it includes the scoped interpretation path.

---

# 16. Networking Version Control

When a peer sends a version, it should send:

```text
carrier file diff
normalized .imo declaration
receipt candidate
parent receipt
skill references
algorithm hashes
projection signature
```

Receiver path:

```text
receive version packet
 ↓
verify carrier hash
 ↓
resolve FS/GS/RS/US scope
 ↓
load required skill definitions
 ↓
validate declaration
 ↓
compare parent receipt
 ↓
accept or reject locally
```

The receiver does not trust the sender.

The receiver validates.

This gives decentralized version control.

```text
send does not accept
receive does not accept
validation accepts
receipt records
```

---

# 17. LLM Version-Control Resolution

For LLMs, this is powerful.

An LLM often edits files by pattern.

OMI version control forces the edit into a scoped relation.

Example prompt:

```text
Update the Base64 seed object.
```

Resolver path:

```text
read AGENTS.md
 ↓
find allowed edit behavior

read SKILLS.md
 ↓
find base64 seed algorithm

resolve scope
 ↓
fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64

create version delta
 ↓
candidate relation

validate
 ↓
tests + scope + algorithm hash

receipt
 ↓
accepted version
```

The model does not simply “change code.”

It proposes a scoped version transition.

The accepted transition becomes receipt.

This makes LLM file editing reproducible.

---

# 18. The Perfect Version-Control Rule

```text
A version is not a file state.
A version is an accepted scoped relation over a carrier file.
```

Therefore:

```text
file diff = carrier delta
OMI declaration = relation delta
skill algorithm = computation delta
Betti/Schläfli = topology delta
receipt = accepted version delta
```

This lets the system track:

```text
what changed physically
what changed semantically
what changed algorithmically
what changed topologically
what was accepted canonically
```

---

# 19. Minimal Version-Control Declaration Format

A minimal OMI version declaration:

```lisp
(version . receipt-2820)
(version.parent . receipt-2819)
(version.scope . fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64/us.o/schema)
(version.seed . o.base64.o)
(version.route . omi-base-64-imo)
(version.carrier . SKILLS.md)
(version.algorithm . skill.base64.zero-polynomial)
(version.schlafli . "{3,3}")
(version.betti . "(1 0 0)")
(version.effect . pure)
(version.result . accepted)
```

A proposed change:

```lisp
(delta.intent . update-base64-schema)
(delta.parent . receipt-2819)
(delta.scope . fs.o/SKILLS.md/gs.o/encoding/rs.o/base64/us.o/schema)
(delta.seed . o.base64.o)
(delta.route . omi-base-64-imo)
(delta.effect . pure)
(delta.status . candidate)
```

Accepted change:

```lisp
(receipt.delta . receipt-2820)
(receipt.parent . receipt-2819)
(receipt.accepts . delta.intent)
(receipt.result . accepted)
```

---

# 20. Canon Statement

OMI version control treats every version as a scoped, validated relation.

`o.o` is the neutral origin object.

Objects such as `o.base64.o` are scoped seed models that define neutral encoding interpretation surfaces.

Routes such as `omi-base-64-imo` declare how the seed moves between citation and carrier.

FS/GS/RS/US provide the place-value scope hierarchy.

CIDR-like prefixes locate versioned definitions inside files and `.imo` carriers.

SKILLS.md defines the exact algorithms that make the relation reproducible.

AGENTS.md defines which version transitions are allowed.

Betti numbers describe the topology of the accepted relation.

Schläfli symbols describe the projected geometric class.

Receipts accept version transitions.

Therefore a version is not merely a file snapshot.

A version is an accepted scoped relation over a carrier file, interpreted through a seed object, validated by deterministic skills, and projected through topology.

---

# 21. One-Sentence Version

OMI version control uses `o.o` seed objects such as `o.base64.o`, citation routes such as `omi-base-64-imo`, FS/GS/RS/US place-value scopes, deterministic SKILLS.md algorithms, AGENTS.md permission environments, and Betti–Schläfli projection signatures so that every version becomes an accepted receipt of a scoped relation rather than merely a text snapshot.

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-05 23:10` | `id:260705-2310` ^260705-231057

What you’ve built here is basically a *multi-layer version control system where “version” is no longer a snapshot, but a typed relation with scope, encoding environment, and topology attached to it*.

If I strip away the language density, I see a fairly coherent stack:

At the bottom you’ve got **place-value addressing (32 slots / control bytes)**, which functions like a positional index space. That’s your “where did change occur” layer.

Above that is **scope segmentation (FS/GS/RS/US)**, which is basically hierarchical namespacing—like CIDR for semantic regions inside a file system.

Then you introduce **seed objects (`o.o`, `o.base64.o`)**, which act like *neutral interpretation contexts*—not data, but a declared decoding/semantics environment.

On top of that is **route encoding (`omi-base-64-imo`)**, which is basically a transport + interpretation contract between two systems.

Then you have **skills (algorithms in SKILLS.md)** and **agents (permissions in AGENTS.md)**, which turn execution into a governed function space rather than an ad-hoc runtime.

Finally you add **receipt-based version identity**, plus **topological signatures (Betti numbers / Schläfli symbols)** to describe structural properties of dependency graphs or transformations.

---

### The key thing you’ve actually converged on (in plain terms)

You’re not really describing “version control with extra math.”

You’re describing:

> **A typed, scoped, reproducible computation ledger over structured transformations of symbolic objects.**

Which is closer to a hybrid of:
- git-like history
- capability-based security systems
- typed lambda calculus / rewrite systems
- and dependency graph topology tracking

---

### Where this becomes interesting (and where it gets fragile)

There are three strong ideas here that *do* have real architectural weight:

1. **Scope-as-first-class metadata**
 - FS/GS/RS/US as semantic nesting is solid
 - This is basically “structured diff domains”

2. **Seed objects as interpretation contexts**
 - This is very close to “execution environments are explicit”
 - Comparable to sandbox + codec + schema bundle

3. **Receipt as semantic commit**
 - This is stronger than hashes because it encodes *why it was valid*, not just what changed

---

Where it starts to strain is:

- Betti/Schläfli mapping is conceptually interesting, but you’d need a *very precise definition of what constitutes the underlying graph* or it becomes metaphor rather than invariant.
- The “32 place-value character space” is doing a lot of symbolic work, but it needs a strict encoding rule if it’s meant to be machine-reconstructable rather than descriptive.
- The OMI/IMO routing layer currently behaves like a naming convention + semantic bus, but it would need formal grammar rules to avoid collapsing into free-form annotation.

---

### If you wanted to make this implementable (minimal path)

You’d probably need to formalize just four things first:

1. **A strict grammar for a version object**
 - what fields are required
 - what is canonical ordering
 - what is hashable vs non-hashable

2. **A definition of “scope resolution”**
 - how FS/GS/RS/US maps to an actual tree or address space

3. **A deterministic interpretation of seed objects**
 - what makes `o.base64.o` different from metadata vs actual runtime config

4. **A precise graph model for topology**
 - what nodes and edges Betti numbers are computed over (dependencies? transformations? receipts?)

---

### A way to reframe your system in one line

If I compress your architecture:

> You are trying to turn “version control” into a **typed semantic execution graph with explicit interpretation environments and formally trackable transformation topology.**

---

If you want, I can help you do one of these next steps:

- reduce this into a minimal formal spec (like a 1–2 page “OMI core kernel”)
- define a strict JSON / Lisp schema for receipts + seeds
- or design how a real parser/interpreter would execute FS/GS/RS/US resolution deterministically

Just tell me which direction you want it to become: *paper system, executable prototype, or mathematical formalism*.
